items.q: What is the difference between brittle and ductile materials?
items.correct: Brittle materials fracture with little or no plastic deformation; ductile materials undergo significant plastic deformation before breaking.
items.misconception: Brittle materials are weak and ductile materials are strong.
items.random_wrong: Brittle materials are metals and ductile materials are non-metals.
items.why: Students conflate brittleness with weakness, missing that a brittle material (like glass) can have very high tensile strength but no warning before failure.

items.q: What determines the de Broglie wavelength of a particle?
items.correct: Its momentum (lambda = h/p) — heavier or faster particles have shorter de Broglie wavelengths.
items.misconception: Its kinetic energy alone, with no dependence on mass.
items.why: Students think the wavelength is a fixed property of the particle type, missing that it depends on momentum and so on speed and mass together.

items.q: How do you see a non-luminous object like a book?
items.correct: Light from a source reflects off the book and enters your eyes.
items.misconception: Your eyes send out beams of vision that hit the book and let you see it.
items.random_wrong: The book emits a special invisible signal that your brain detects directly.
items.why: The 'extramission' model of vision is a stubborn childhood intuition, reinforced by phrases like 'cast your eye over' or comic-book vision rays.
The answer examiners want is: Most atom is empty space. “Plum pudding model” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Transition from n=3 to n=2 in H gives photon of energy 1.89 eV. λ in nm (1 eV = 1.6e-19 J, hc = 1240 eV·nm):
items.why: The real figure is 656. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
